titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Sunset Park Chinese community oral histories and exhibition materials ARC.325
titlestmt.author: Brett Dion, Maria Santiago, and Olivia Mastrosimone
titlestmt.sponsor: Portions of this collection were initially processed and described by Brett Dion and Maria Santiago as part of the project, 'Voices of Generations: Investigating Brooklyn's Cultural Identity,' funded by the National Historical Publications and Records Commission (NHPRC) and The Gladys Krieble Delmas Foundation. Olivia Mastrosimone reprocessed this collection in 2026.

did.unittitle: Sunset Park Chinese community oral histories and exhibition materials

physdesc.extent: in 210 files, total running time: 43 hours, 7 minutes, 27 seconds (series 1); in three record boxes
in 5 manuscript boxes, 1 oversize flat box, 3 audiocassette boxes, and 2 O-ring album boxes.

did.abstract: This collection includes oral histories, photographs, and print materials collected and donated for exhibitions documenting the Chinese community in Brooklyn's Sunset Park neighborhood.
did.materialspec: The digitized portions of this collection are available on our oral history portal.
did.langmaterial: Materials in Mandarin, Cantonese, English, Spanish, and Turkish.

bioghist.head: Historical note
bioghist.p: The materials in this collection encompass an oral history project and exhibitions that were part of a joint effort between the Brooklyn Historical Society and what was then the Chinatown History Museum (now the Museum of Chinese in America). The collaboration started with the oral history project, for which the two organizations received a grant from the National Endowment for the Humanities and additional funding from the Ford Foundation. Awarded in 1992, the grant endeavored to explore how the new community of Chinese immigrants centered at Brooklyn's Eighth Avenue took root and blossomed during the 1980s. By the early 1990s, the community boasted a population of approximately 60,000 people and was known as New York City's third Chinatown.

The project's initial co-directors, Ellen M. Snyder-Grenier of the Brooklyn Historical Society and Mary Ting Yi Lui of the Chinatown History Museum designed it to create primary documentation of the new ethnic community at a moment when many of its pioneers were still in place. They hoped to answer who these new community members were and what political and economic forces brought them to Brooklyn. By July of 1993, the project's staff had restructured to include coordinator Gregory Ruf and lead interviewer Ka-Kam Chui. Fabiana Chiu of Brooklyn Historical Society provided additional assistance in interviews. Project interviewers recorded a total of thirty-one interviews from past and present neighborhood residents of Chinese, Italian, Norwegian, and Hispanic descent. The group represents a diverse cohort in respect to age, place of origin, socio-economic position, native language, and date of immigration.

After completing the oral histories in 1994, the two organizations began planning and collecting materials for a larger exhibition highlighting Sunset Park's Chinese community. In addition to the oral histories, the exhibition would present the photographs, contemporary objects, artifacts, and performances relating to the oral histories and the social and cultural processes they document. Ka-Kam Chui and Gregory Ruf provided the majority of donated materials, and the Brooklyn Historical Society hired photographers Nina Kuo, Rex Chen, and Charleen Chan to take photos for display.

The BHS exhibition, New Neighbors: Sunset Park's Chinese Community, opened in 1996, and the Chinatown History Museum exhibition, A Good Place to Land One's Feet: Brooklyn's New Chinese Community, opened in 1999.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This collection is organized into three series:

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This collection includes oral histories, photographs, and print materials collected and donated for exhibitions documenting the Chinese community in Brooklyn's Sunset Park neighborhood.

Thirty-one oral history interviews, originally recorded on portable cassette tapes, were conducted documenting the experiences of both Chinese and non-Chinese community members whose homes clustered around Eighth Avenue in the Sunset Park neighborhood of Brooklyn. While the ethnically-Chinese narrators were chosen to represent both early (pre-1985) and late (post-1985) waves of immigration, people of Hispanic, Norwegian, and Italian heritage were chosen to represent the different ethnic communities that shared the neighborhood. The oral histories contain descriptions of immigration, living arrangements, neighborhood ethnicities, discrimination, employment, community development, political leadership, petty and organized crime, and adjustment to American life.

BHS and CHM hired photographers to document both daily life and cultural and community events in Sunset Park, including the Lunar New Year Parade, Brooklyn Chinese American Association events, and cultural fairs, as well as day-to-day life on and around Eighth Avenue. Photographers also took portraits of oral history interviewees and photographed the opening of the BHS exhibition in 1996.

Materials donated to and collected by BHS and CHM include flyers and brochures for cultural and professional organizations, religious literature, posters, business cards and advertisements from Sunset Park businesses, and Chinese-language newspapers and newsletters.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Open to researchers with varied restrictions according to narrator agreement. Oral histories can be accessed onsite at the Center for Brooklyn History's Othmer Library and online on the Oral History Portal. Administrative materials are open to researchers upon request and are accessible onsite at the Othmer Library. Interview transcripts and interview A/V materials are not available for research.
Other collection materials are open to users without restriction onsite at the Othmer Library.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: Use of the oral histories other than for private study, scholarship, or research requires the permission of the Center for Brooklyn History. For assistance, please consult library staff at cbhreference@bklynlibrary.org.

While many items at the Center for Brooklyn History are unrestricted, we do not own reproduction rights to all materials. Be aware of the several kinds of rights that might apply: copyright, licensing, and trademarks. The researcher assumes all responsibility for copyright questions.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Identification of item, date (if known); Sunset Park Chinese community oral histories and exhibition materials, ARC.325, [Box and Folder number]; Brooklyn Public Library, Center for Brooklyn History.

[Narrator Last Name, First name], Oral history interview conducted by [Interviewer First name Last name], Interview Date [Month day, YYYY], Sunset Park Chinese community oral histories and exhibition materials, ARC.325 [Object ID]; Brooklyn Public Library, Center for Brooklyn History.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Materials contained in this collection were acquired by the Brooklyn Historical Society and the Chinatown History Museum.

altformavail.head: Existence and Location of Copies
altformavail.p: This oral history collection is duplicated and stored on-site at MOCA's Collections & Research Center.

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: In addition to this collection, the Center for Brooklyn History has other oral history collections related to residents of Sunset Park:
• Puerto Rican Oral History Project records, 1960-1984 (1976.001)
Project records are available in the:
• Brooklyn Historical Society institutional records, ARC.288
Oral history collections related to the Chinese American community located at the Museum of Chinese in America include:
• Archaeology of Change: Mapping Tales of Gentrification in New York City's Chinatown

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Series 1 was initially digitized and cataloged at the item level by Alexis Taines and Niles French, oral history interns, in 2009, and Sady Sullivan, Director of Oral History, in 2010. Series 1 was compressed for streaming, uploaded, and described by Maria Santiago and Margaret May, project interns, and Brett Dion, project archivist, in summer 2016. Many oral histories in Cantonese and Mandarin were described and indexed by Museum of Chinese in America (MOCA) staff in 2016, including Joy He, Yiyang Li, Yue Ma, Gengwu Wang, Chongyuan Wang, and Siwei Wang. Due to privacy concerns, the specific dates of birth of all narrators or other named individuals were redacted from the accessible transcripts and audio recordings. Olivia Mastrosimone, Project Archivist, finished processing the collection in 2026.

Folder titles in brackets were provided by Olivia Mastrosimone. The archivist moved administrative materials and photo duplicates from this collection to ARC.288 Brooklyn Historical Society institutional records, and discarded additional digital copies of oral history interviews on floppy disks.

odd.head: Oral History Note
odd.p: Oral history interviews are intimate conversations between two people, both of whom have generously agreed to share these recordings with the Center for Brooklyn History archives and with researchers. Please listen in the spirit with which these were shared. Researchers will understand that:

1. The Center for Brooklyn History abides by the General Principles & Best Practices for Oral History as agreed upon by the Oral History Association (2009) and expects that use of this material will be done with respect for these professional ethics.

2. Every oral history relies on the memories, views, and opinions of the narrator. Because of the personal nature of oral history, listeners may find some viewpoints or language of the recorded participants to be objectionable. In keeping with its mission of preservation and unfettered access whenever possible, CBH presents these views as recorded.

3. Transcripts created prior to 2008 serve as a guide to the interview and are not considered verbatim. The audio recording should be considered the primary source for each interview. It may contain natural false starts, verbal stumbles, misspeaks, repetitions that are common in conversation, and other passages and phrases omitted from the transcript. This decision was made because CBH gives primacy to the audible voice and also because some researchers do find useful information in these verbal patterns.

4. Unless these verbal patterns are germane to your scholarly work, when quoting from this material, researchers are encouraged to correct the grammar and make other modifications, maintaining the flavor of the narrator's speech while editing the material for the standards of print.

altformavail.head: Existence and Location of Copies

dao.xlink:title: Oral History Interview (Audio) with Anonymous

daodesc.p: Oral History Interview (Audio) with Anonymous

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Born in 1964 to a land-owning family of the educated elite, the narrator's family fortune was reversed during the Chinese Cultural Revolution. At twenty-two, she immigrated to America to attend Clarion University in Pennsylvania. After graduation, she moved to New York City and found a job as a librarian in Brooklyn. She later married a Chinese man and helped her parents and sister immigrate to New York City from Toronto, Canada. At the time of the interview, the narrator felt stuck between two cultures she loved.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: In this interview, the narrator discusses Chinese assimilation into American culture by contrasting her own experiences with those of her younger sister and of her parents. She notes class and lifestyle differences among the different Chinese immigrants of New York City and delineates them by their home provinces, their education levels, their native language, and "old" versus "new" immigrants, while also considering "ABCs" or American-Born Chinese. She discusses ethnic tensions between the Chinese American and Hispanic American residents of the Sunset Park neighborhood of Brooklyn. The narrator also states that she feels she has become culturally American. Interview conducted by Gregory Ruf.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access and Use
accessrestrict.p: A/V materials and interview transcripts are not available for research use. Access is available onsite at the Center for Brooklyn History's Othmer Library and the Oral History Portal. Use of the oral histories other than for private study, scholarship, or research requires the permission of CBH. For assistance, contact digitalcollections@bklynlibrary.org.

dao.xlink:title: Oral History Interview (Audio) with Anonymous

daodesc.p: Oral History Interview (Audio) with Anonymous

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: The narrator was born circa 1950 in Taishan, Mainland China. After a brief stay in Hong Kong, the family settled in Saigon, Vietnam, where she lived for thirty years. As an adult, she worked, married, and had five children. The family lived comfortably until the end of the Vietnam War, when the Việt Cộng took over South Vietnam and took possession of her family's considerable financial portfolio. Forced to flee, the narrator and her children were granted refugee status in America and settled in Brooklyn. She started numerous entrepreneurial pursuits, including a real estate business. At the time of the 1993 interview, the narrator lived in Brooklyn with her husband and four adult children.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: In this interview, the narrator tells her life story and includes details on generations of her family's history. She describes living in Saigon, the Việt Cộng takeover, fleeing the country with her family, and immigrating to America as a refugee. The narrator discusses her entrepreneurial pursuits by evaluating each business that she started and the reasons for their respective failures. She discusses the real estate business in the Sunset Park neighborhood of Brooklyn, providing general rent information and identifying prospects for the area and its Chinese-American residents. Interview in Mandarin conducted by Gregory Ruf.

dao.xlink:title: Oral History Interview (Audio) with Anonymous

daodesc.p: Oral History Interview (Audio) with Anonymous

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: The narrator, in her forties when the interview occurred in 1993, was born in Mainland China and moved with her family to Kowloon, Hong Kong when she was a young girl. The daughter of schoolteachers from China's educated class, she was educated in private schools, nurtured a love of painting, and studied English in college. In the 1960s, she moved with her husband to New York City and ultimately settled in the Sunset Park neighborhood of Brooklyn. A mother of four, the narrator also worked outside the home as a typist, then a bridal gown seamstress, and eventually the owner of a small art gallery.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: In this interview, the narrator provides an overview of her life both before and after immigrating to New York City from Hong Kong. She discusses her childhood and private schooling in Kowloon, her decision to move to America with her husband, and her trip to New York City in the 1960s. The narrator recounts the evolution of her career from typist to bridal gown seamstress, to art gallery owner. She offers her thoughts on parenting and practical home economics and provides an overview of the financial situation faced by Chinese immigrants and small business owners. Interview in Mandarin conducted by Gregory Ruf.

Access is available onsite at the Center for Brooklyn History's Othmer Library and the Oral History Portal. Use of the oral histories other than for private study, scholarship, or research requires the permission of CBH. For assistance, contact digitalcollections@bklynlibrary.org.

dao.xlink:title: Oral History Interview (Audio) with Louis Castaldo

daodesc.p: Oral History Interview (Audio) with Louis Castaldo

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: The son of an Italian father and an Italian-American mother, Louis Castaldo (nicknamed Lou) was thirty-nine years old in 1994. During his childhood, he lived in the Bensonhurst neighborhood of Brooklyn and attended public schools within the area. In the Sunset Park neighborhood of Brooklyn, Castaldo's father, Vinnie, bought and ran Vinnie's Pizzeria as a family business from 1970 onward. Castaldo worked alongside his father at the pizzeria from his early teens to the time of the interview in 1994.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: In this interview, Lou Castaldo discusses his father's business, Vinnie's Pizzeria. His father opened the restaurant in the early 1970s in the Sunset Park neighborhood of Brooklyn. He recounts his father's coming to America and life before starting the business. Castaldo describes the various cultures and neighborhoods he observed while growing up in Brooklyn, including significant events, activities, hobbies, family life, and traditions. Castaldo recalls a significant period of change in Sunset Park that began in 1979, noting its shift from a European-American neighborhood comprised of Germans, Greeks, Irish, Italians, and Norwegians, to an Asian-American neighborhood of largely Chinese heritage. He evaluates other influences that factored into the changing neighborhood, such as escalating crime rates and drug use, business closures, new Chinese-American ownership of businesses and housing, cultural differences and language barriers, and national politics supporting undocumented immigrantion into the United States. He surveys the Sunset Park community's actions to create a more global and shared sense of community. Interview conducted by Gregory Ruf.

dao.xlink:title: Oral History Interview (Audio) with Guo Wa (Grace) Chan

daodesc.p: Oral History Interview (Audio) with Guo Wa (Grace) Chan

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Born in Guangzhou, China, in 1955, Guo Wa (Grace) Chan immigrated to New York City in 1981 to join her husband, who had immigrated to America almost a decade earlier. In 1985, Chan opened Prosperity Video, the first Chinese-language movie rental store in the Chinatown area within the Sunset Park neighborhood of Brooklyn, because she realized she could care for her new baby while running the shop. At the time of the 1993 interview, Chan was a busy mother of four children. While she worked 364 days a year at the shop, in the evenings, she was an active parent with a particular interest in her children's progress at school. Chan looked forward to one day taking her children on vacation to Florida.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: In this interview, Guo Wa (Grace) Chan discusses her immigration to America and life in New York City. She focuses on her experiences as a businesswoman and details the launch of her video rental store. She mentions her husband's ventures in the restaurant industry. Chan describes the entrepreneurial competition in Brooklyn's Chinatown and real estate prices, both commercial and residential. As an educationally-focused mother, Chan evaluates the educational decisions she has made and the quality of local public and private schools. Interview in Cantonese conducted by Mary Lui.

dao.xlink:title: Oral History Interview (Audio) with Kwok-Wai (David) Chan

daodesc.p: Oral History Interview (Audio) with Kwok-Wai (David) Chan

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Kwok-Wai (David) Chan was born in Mainland China in 1955. In 1975, Chan emigrated with his family from Hong Kong to New York City and immediately settled in Brooklyn. He was nineteen at the time. Chan received a Bachelor's degree in Accounting from the State University of New York at Buffalo. After college, Chan eventually found his vocation as an entrepreneur. At the time of the 1993 interview, Chan was the owner of a pharmacy in the Sunset Park neighborhood of Brooklyn that catered to the local Chinese-American immigrants. Chan was a married father to two daughters. In 1993, the family lived in a newly purchased home in Staten Island.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: In this interview, Kwok-Wai (David) Chan relates his experience as a Chinese immigrant to New York City. He describes his reasons for emigrating, the family's arrival in Brooklyn, and the hardships faced soon after immigration. He discusses his education and the value his family places on higher education—something he calls a Chinese value. Chan details his career as an entrepreneur, including failures, challenges, and accomplishments. Throughout the interview, Chan discusses the community changes he's observed over the last few decades. Interview conducted by Mary Lui.

dao.xlink:title: Oral History Interview (Audio) with Yan Chen

daodesc.p: Oral History Interview (Audio) with Yan Chen

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Yan Chen was born in 1974 in Mainland China. Her family moved to the United States in 1983 when Chen was about nine years old. They lived in the Sunset Park neighborhood of Brooklyn in a building their grandfather owned, as well as the Chinatown neighborhood of Manhattan. Chen went to elementary school in Manhattan's Chinatown for three years before the family moved back to Sunset Park. Her parents worked in Manhattan Chinatown. Chen attended Robert F. Wagner Middle School and Murry Bergtraum High School in Manhattan. When she was a teenager, her father got a job at Bay Ridge Restaurant in Sunset Park, and her mother worked in a factory in Brooklyn. Chen also worked for a brief time sewing clothes in a factory. At the time of the 1993 interview, Chen was a young adult exploring her professional and personal worlds.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: In this interview, Yan Chen talks about her life in Mainland China, where she was born and grew up, including descriptions of the family home, farm, her family, extended family, and many farm animals. Chen recalls how, at age ten, she and her family came to the United States with the help of extended family. She remembers that her parents hoped to make a lot of money in America without working too hard. The interview mainly consists of Chen discussing her relationships to the two areas where she lived (the Chinatown neighborhood of Manhattan and the Sunset Park neighborhood of Brooklyn) and her sense of place in each. She describes her family experiences, her education and friendships, demographic shifts within the neighborhoods, problems with bullying, crime and gangs, Chinese home ownership, and the establishment of Chinese businesses—all factors which helped to stabilize the Chinese community and allowed her family to prosper. Interview conducted by Mary Lui.

dao.xlink:title: Oral History Interview (Audio) with Huan Reng (Benson) Chin

daodesc.p: Oral History Interview (Audio) with Huan Reng (Benson) Chin

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Huan Reng (Benson) Chin was born in 1922 to a merchant-class Chinese family in Bangkok, Thailand. When the Sino-Japanese war broke out, Chin, aged fourteen, became a spy for the Japanese Resistance Committee of Overseas Chinese. Chin experienced World War II, the Cultural Revolution, and the Chinese Communist Party as a spy and revolutionary. In 1984, sixty-two-year-old Chin immigrated to New York City, where he found work as a dishwasher in Manhattan's Chinatown. He later moved to Brooklyn's Chinatown in the Sunset Park neighborhood of Brooklyn. At the time of the 1993 interview, Chin looked forward to retirement.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: In this interview, Huan Reng (Benson) Chin discusses his involvement in the Sino-Japanese war and his espionage for the Chinese Communist Party. He recalls his military education in Marxism-Leninism and his service in World War II. Chin speaks of the Chinese Cultural Revolution, including detention in labor camps, mass starvation, and numerous civilian suicides. Chin evaluates life after his 1984 immigration to New York City, discussing Chinatowns in the boroughs, Sunset Park street crime, and his anticipation of retirement. Interview in Cantonese conducted by Mary Lui.

dao.xlink:title: Oral History Interview (Audio) with Wong Lai Chow

daodesc.p: Oral History Interview (Audio) with Wong Lai Chow

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Wong Lai Chow was born in 1920 in Guangzhou, China. Although her parents moved to America when Chow was very young, Chow remained in China. As an adult, she married, had three children, joined the National People's Party (KMT), and earned a degree in education from Zhongshan University. During the Chinese Cultural Revolution, Chow's mother attempted to rescue her from China. For this, Chow suffered years of public humiliation and three years of hard labor. After her mother successfully sponsored her visa application in 1980, Chow brought her family to America. At the time of the 1993 interview, Chow was an active retiree in a senior hospital apartment in the Sunset Park neighborhood of Brooklyn.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: In this interview, Wong Lai Chow describes her life, spanning the Japanese invasion of China, World War II, the Korean War, the Chinese Cultural Revolution, and her immigration to America in 1980. She recalls her career as an educator during the Chinese Cultural Revolution, being charged with "illicit relations with a foreign country," and re-education at a hard labor camp. Chow also speaks from her perspective as an active retiree in the Sunset Park neighborhood of Brooklyn. She discusses neighborhood crime, the Chinese mafia, and unlicensed dentists of Sunset Park. Chow also observes the challenges faced by her children, who came to America without command of English or many marketable skills. Interview conducted in Cantonese by Ka-Kam Chui.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Yui Man (Michael) Chow immigrated to New York City with his family in March of 1991 at age twelve. Chow is the youngest of three children. At the time of the 1993 interview, Chow had been in Brooklyn for just two years. Still, his experiences had made him confident of his future in New York City, and he did not wish to return to China.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: In this interview, Yui Man (Michael) Chow describes his journey to America at age twelve. He discusses his experiences of adversity, homesickness, missing family members, learning English, assimilating to American culture and the American education system, traveling and sightseeing, playing ball games, and receiving an American name. As he makes friends, Chow says he enjoys America and favors it over China. Interview in Cantonese conducted by Mary Lui.

did.materialspec: This interviewee's name is June Flodsand. She wished to remain anonymous.

dao.xlink:title: Oral History Interview (Audio) with June Flodsand

daodesc.p: Oral History Interview (Audio) with June Flodsand

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: The narrator was born circa 1942 in New York City to Norwegian immigrant parents. Raised in a working-class family, she lived on 58th Street in the Sunset Park neighborhood of Brooklyn from 1958 onward. She is the eldest of six children. Her life has been spent in equal parts hard work, socializing at neighborhood soda shops and ice cream parlors, and activities at the Lutheran Church. After graduating from high school, the narrator married. She has spent her life as an observant Lutheran and proud Norwegian American. Remaining a Sunset Park resident, she called 59th Street home at the time of the 1994 interview.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: In this interview, the narrator recounts her life as a working-class, first-generation American and a devout Lutheran of Norwegian parentage. She remembers a childhood of family chores, a first job as a soda jerk, and weekly participation in church and its activities. As an adult, the narrator married a fellow Lutheran and continued a life devoted to work, church, Norwegian-themed festivities, and socializing. The narrator laments the transition of the Brooklyn neighborhood of Sunset Park from predominantly Scandinavian to largely Chinese and Hasidic. She was interviewed at the Lutheran Church, where she is a member. Interview conducted by Gregory Ruf.

dao.xlink:title: Oral History Interview (Audio) with Tony Giordano

daodesc.p: Oral History Interview (Audio) with Tony Giordano

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Born in 1948, Tony Giordano spent his pre-adolescent years in the Carroll Gardens neighborhood of Brooklyn. He grew up surrounded by a family of Italian heritage with three siblings, as well as Italian American and Irish American friends and neighbors. In 1961, the family moved to Sunset Park, Brooklyn, a neighborhood with a strong Puerto Rican heritage and an influx of Irish and Italian Americans. Giordano attended Brooklyn Technical High School and Staten Island Community College. After a brief foray into engineering work with the New York City Transit Authority, he became a teacher. His civic-minded upbringing led to a role as a neighborhood leader in Sunset Park.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: By outlining the experiences of the two prior generations, Tony Giordano explains in the interview how his family of Italian heritage came to be in Brooklyn. He focuses on his father's occupations, especially his job as a bus driver. Giordano describes his upbringing in terms of his family's apartment housing, Brooklyn neighborhoods, vacations on Long Island, education from first grade through college, and his Roman Catholic religious experience. During the interview, Giordano discusses in-depth his experience of losing faith in organized religion, and later discovering a devotion to God. Contributing to this struggle with religion, Giordano talks about a failed first marriage, custody challenges over his son, a rekindled relationship, and a happy, interfaith marriage and family. He focuses on the ethnic heritage and the late twentieth century makeup of the Sunset Park community and his role as a civic leader. The influx of large numbers of Chinese into Sunset Park in the late 1980s has created what Giordano calls the "Third Chinatown." Interview conducted by Gregory Ruf.

dao.xlink:title: Oral History Interview (Audio) with Mak Shui Ka

daodesc.p: Oral History Interview (Audio) with Mak Shui Ka

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Mak Shui Ka was born in Naahn Sehk Village, in Guangdong, China, to a wealthy, prominent family. She grew up with servants and attended the country's most prestigious schools. Her family's fortunes reversed during the opium suppression effort, which drove the family business into the ground, and the Communist liberation, which repossessed her family's estate and persecuted her father. Ka relocated to America and eventually settled with her husband and three children in New York City. In Brooklyn, Ka took on odd jobs and experienced poverty for the first time. She persevered, joined the Seamstresses' Union and the Coalition of Women, and became a community organizer. As an organizer, she championed the Sunset Park neighborhood of Brooklyn's Chinese community and pursued anti-discrimination activity. At the time of the 1993 interview, Ka lived happily in Sunset Park with her husband, with her three adult children living nearby.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: In this interview, Mak Shui Ka discusses her life's history in Communist China, including her upbringing, schools, her family, and persecution, as well as her successes as a conscripted cadet. Ka describes her life in America, which brought her first experiences with poverty and grave anti-Chinese discrimination. She describes anti-Chinese treatment by Italian members of the Sunset Park neighborhood of Brooklyn. In response, Ka helped organize a community protest event and delivered a speech that vowed constant vigilance. She details extensively another incident of discrimination in which police stormed an apartment in the Chinatown neighborhood of Manhattan, then beat the family inside, including a pregnant woman. Ka was called as a community first responder, which involved taking photographs and witness testimony, and helping to prosecute the police officers involved. In relation to Sunset Park, Ka also references her real estate transactions, the rent prices and taxes, and neighborhood crime. Interview in Cantonese conducted by Ka-Kam Chui.

dao.xlink:title: Oral History Interview (Audio) with Harrison Kang

daodesc.p: Oral History Interview (Audio) with Harrison Kang

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Harrison Kang and his family immigrated to America from South Korea in 1980. Kang attended high school and college in New York City. After a brief stint at a job on Long Island, he returned to Brooklyn to work in his parents' dry cleaning shop. At the time of the interview, Harrison Kang was thirty-one years old.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This brief interview was conducted at the Kang family's dry cleaning shop, located at 5214 Eighth Avenue in Brooklyn. In the interview, Harrison Kang mentions his family's emigration from South Korea to New York City. He describes the evolution of the Brooklyn neighborhood of Sunset Park, from relative quiet to a bustling area of immigrants and small businesses. Kang discusses the neighborhood's change from a mix of Norwegian and Hispanic residents to a primarily Chinese population. Kang provides an overview of the ethnic groups residing on specific neighborhood streets. Interview conducted by Mary Lui.

dao.xlink:title: Oral History Interview (Audio) with Fai Ling (Alice) Lee

daodesc.p: Oral History Interview (Audio) with Fai Ling (Alice) Lee

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Fai Ling (Alice) Lee was born in Mainland China circa the 1950s, moved to Hong Kong at age three, and immigrated to New York City at fifteen. Her family initially lived in the Bronx before settling in the Sunset Park neighborhood of Brooklyn. She attended Hunter College and worked in the New York City municipal finance department before taking time off to have children and raise a family. During her seven-year maternity leave, Lee nurtured her interest in real estate and began part-time work at a real estate agency. At the time of the interview, Lee worked in bilingual education at P.S. 314 in Sunset Park.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: In this interview, Fai Ling (Alice) Lee discusses the development of the Sunset Park neighborhood of Brooklyn from a sleepy majority-Norwegian area in the 1970s to a thriving Chinese community in the 1980s and 1990s. She describes the economic and working conditions faced by her father, who worked in a Chinese restaurant in Times Square, and her mother, a seamstress in the Chinatown neighborhood of Manhattan. The interview focuses on real estate investing in Sunset Park, including home prices, mortgages, rental income, and risks. Lee also mentions her position as a bilingual educator at P.S. 314 in Sunset Park. Interview in Cantonese conducted by Ka-Kam Chui.

dao.xlink:title: Oral History Interview (Audio) with Johnny Lee

daodesc.p: Oral History Interview (Audio) with Johnny Lee

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Johnny Lee (pseudonym) was born circa 1961 in Hong Kong, China, the youngest of five siblings. He immigrated to New York City as a teenager and graduated from Seward Park High School in the Chinatown neighborhood of Manhattan. The son of a working-class family, Lee sewed buttons in a Chinatown garment factory during high school and washed dishes at a Chinatown restaurant as a young man. He attended City College for one year before leaving to pursue stable government work at an Off-Track Betting branch. At the time of the interview, Lee was in his forties and lived with his wife and three children in the three-family home that his parents purchased and converted into a five-family house.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: In this interview, Johnny Lee (pseudonym) recounts his life history. He recollects being raised in Hong Kong by his mother while his father worked in America. He remembers his time at Chinatown's Seward Park High School's bilingual program, classmates who dropped out to join Chinese gangs, after-school work as a button-sewer in garment factories, college work as a dishwasher at a Chinese restaurant, and the decision to leave City College to pursue full-time work at an Off-Track Betting location. Lee discusses the boom in Manhattan's Chinatown garment factories, the rise in competition, and the resulting deflation in real wages and living standards. He talks about working-class income, real estate prices, his family's decision to purchase a home, and the rejuvenation of the Sunset Park neighborhood of Brooklyn and its Eighth Avenue small businesses. Interview in Cantonese conducted by Ka-Kam Chui.

dao.xlink:title: Oral History Interview with Hoen-Yee (Curie) Leung

daodesc.p: Oral History Interview with Hoen-Yee (Curie) Leung

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Hoen-Yee (Curie) Leung immigrated to New York City from Hong Kong as a young adult and moved to the Sunset Park neighborhood of Brooklyn in the late 1980s. A single woman in her forties, Leung's life revolved around her career in radio broadcasting at the Chung Wah Commercial Broadcasting Company. When not working, she spent time singing karaoke and Cantonese opera with the Man Chi Opera Society, and attending Sunset Park's traditional Chinese festivals.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: In this interview, Hoen-Yee (Curie) Leung describes her life as a single, female Chinese immigrant living alone in the Sunset Park neighborhood of Brooklyn. Hoen recounts the process of getting a job as a disk jockey at a Chinese radio station, and her promotion to supervisor of the Programming Department. She discusses neighborhood ethnic relations and crime (including being mugged). Leung tells of her leisure activities, such as singing karaoke and opera, attending Sunset Park's traditional Chinese festivals, and watching soap operas. Interview in Cantonese conducted by Ka-Kam Chui.

dao.xlink:title: Oral History Interview (Audio) with Yee Ming Leung

daodesc.p: Oral History Interview (Audio) with Yee Ming Leung

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Yee Ming Leung was born in Hong Kong, China, circa 1958. He immigrated to America at age eleven and settled in the Bay Ridge neighborhood of Brooklyn. Leung attended public schools during school integration, an experience that shaped his views of the American melting pot. After attending the University of the Pacific, Leung spent ten years in California as a teacher and owner of an import-export business. He returned to New York City to help his sister's growing restaurant business. Leung became a restaurateur himself with the opening of Oriental Palace in the Sunset Park neighborhood of Brooklyn. At the time of the 1993 interview, Leung sat on Community Board 4 and had recently lost his first campaign for a seat on the school board of District 20.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: In this interview, Yee Ming Leung describes his teenage assimilation into American culture, discussing a childhood of "Chinese" activities such as catching crabs on Coney Island or stealing nuts from neighbors' trees, as well as playing baseball and watching football or movies with his new American friends. Leung recalls his life as a businessman and later as a restaurateur. He discusses his campaign and motivations for running for a seat on the School Board, Chinese attitudes towards voting, and his activities as an activist and member of Community Board 4. Interview in Cantonese conducted by Ka-Kam Chui.

dao.xlink:title: Oral History Interview (Audio) with Miu Fei Li

daodesc.p: Oral History Interview (Audio) with Miu Fei Li

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Miu Fei Li was born in Mainland China in 1959. In 1981, she followed her husband to New York City and settled in the Chinatown neighborhood of Manhattan. She became a professional seamstress, joined the local seamstressess' union, and had two children. After finding work out of state, Li's husband developed a gambling addiction. After racking up tens of thousands of dollars in debt, they divorced. In 1993, Li married a fellow divorcee whom she met while on vacation in Hong Kong. At the time of the interview that same year, Miu Fei Li was living with her new husband and their combined four children in the Sunset Park neighborhood of Brooklyn.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: In this interview, Miu Fei Li discusses life since her 1981 immigration to New York City. She tells of the decision to immigrate to America as a twenty-two year-old newlywed, initial impressions of Manhattan, and early attempts to learn English. She describes life and working conditions experienced as a garment factory seamstress and the benefits of being a union member. She talks about her work schedule, which she believes played a role in fostering her husband's gambling addiction and their subsequent divorce. Li speaks of her second marriage. She also evaluates real estate conditions in the Sunset Park neighborhood of Brooklyn circa 1993. Interview in Cantonese conducted by Ka-Kam Chui.

dao.xlink:title: Oral History Interview (Audio) with Ning-Yuan Li and Anonymous

daodesc.p: Oral History Interview (Audio) with Ning-Yuan Li and Anonymous

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Ning-Yuan Li was born in 1936 in Nanjing, China, to an educated family, and as a teenager became an award-winning fine arts painter. At the time of the interview, he had been in America for four years and had a school-aged daughter. J.L. (Anonymous) was born in Burma, but soon moved to Taiwan. During the "Anti-Japan War," J.L. was orphaned and subsequently joined the fight against the Japanese invasion. Later, he moved to Paraguay and Brazil, where he started a small wristwatch business. He immigrated to America in 1984. At the time of the interview, he had a daughter in kindergarten. J.T. (Anonymous) also provides narration. Her own full-length oral history (1994.007.03) is available as part of this collection.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: In this interview, J.L. (Anonymous) and Ning-Yuan Li offer perspectives on the different paths that led them both to America. Li, a fine arts painter, describes his early education and artistic sensibilities, and recalls the Chinese Cultural Revolution's impact on fine art in China. J.L., from Taiwan, relates his life story; the era of the "Anti-Japan War," his career as a soldier, and time spent in Paraguay running a small wrist-watch business. Joined by J.T. (Anonymous, also a narrator of her own oral history), J.L. and Li discuss their shared experience as residents of Brooklyn's Chinatown, working conditions for Chinese immigrants, language barriers, armed robberies, and the American education and juvenile corrections systems. Interview in Mandarin conducted by Gregory Ruf.

dao.xlink:title: Oral History Interview (Audio) with Paul Mak

daodesc.p: Oral History Interview (Audio) with Paul Mak

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Paul Mak was born in Hong Kong, China, and immigrated to New York City at around age sixteen. Priced out of the Chinatown neighborhood of Manhattan, his family settled into Brooklyn. After completing high school in Brooklyn, Mak graduated from Hunter College with a degree in computer hardware programming. Unable to quickly find a job in programming, Mak entered into a career in civil service. In 1988, Mak helped found the Brooklyn Chinese-American Association, an influential Eighth Avenue-based business services organization. At the time of the 1993 interview, Mak was the only Asian community board member in the Sunset Park neighborhood of Brooklyn.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: In this interview, Paul Mak discusses his personal assimilation into mainstream American culture. He details his career as a civil servant serving the Chinese community of Eighth Avenue in the Brooklyn neighborhood of Sunset Park, helping educate new immigrants assimilate by teaching them American customs, smoothing out relationships with neighboring ethnic groups (particularly the Latino community), and working to develop Brooklyn's Chinatown by facilitating the migration of garment factories from Manhattan to Brooklyn. He also describes his time as a community board member. Interview conducted by Mary Lui.

dao.xlink:title: Oral History Interview (Audio) with Edmundo Quinones

daodesc.p: Oral History Interview (Audio) with Edmundo Quinones

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Edmundo Quinones was born circa 1946 in Manhattan to Puerto Rican immigrant parents and grew up in the Bay Ridge neighborhood of Brooklyn. As Latinos, the Quinones were discriminated against. Still, he remembers a childhood of mixed play in his working-class neighborhood with stick-ball and water balloons. At age fourteen, Quinones moved to Puerto Rico. When he re-settled in New York City, he found the Sunset Park neighborhood of Brooklyn to be inhabited primarily by Latinos. He married a Puerto Rican woman and had two sons. Around 1985, Quinones stumbled upon Brooklyn's Chinatown and was amazed to find the Chinese American community so quickly well-established.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: In this interview, Edmundo Quinones discusses his life as a first-generation working-class Puerto Rican American. He reflects on the challenges he's faced as a Latino growing up in a White neighborhood, particularly discrimination, and strengthening his Latino identity while avoiding mainstream assimilation. He provides an overarching view of the American dream: class mobility (both upward and downward), ethnic relations, and neighborhood change. He describes the Brooklyn neighborhood of Bay Ridge's transformation from a predominantly poor Scandinavian enclave, to a Latino area called Sunset Park, to a bustling, predominantly Chinese American community. Interview conducted by Gregory Ruf and Fabiana Chiu.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access and Use
accessrestrict.p: A/V materials and interview transcripts are not available for research use. Access is available onsite at the Center for Brooklyn History's Othmer Library and the Oral History Portal. Use of the oral histories other than for private study, scholarship, or research requires the permission of CBH. For assistance, contact digitalcollections@bklynlibrary.org.

dao.xlink:title: Oral History Interview (Audio) with Po Yee Ung

daodesc.p: Oral History Interview (Audio) with Po Yee Ung

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Po Yee Ung, born in Hong Kong circa 1957, immigrated to New York City at age twenty-five to accept a job offer. She settled in the Brighton Beach neighborhood of Brooklyn. As a young career woman in Tai Po, China, Ung worked in both the garment and electronics industries. In New York City, she worked primarily as a journalist writing for Chinese-language print newspapers and weeklies, reporting and interviewing for Chinese-language Apple Television, and writing a column for a Chinese-language entertainment weekly. At the time of the 1993 interview, Ung was thirty-six years old, lived alone in the Sunset Park neighborhood of Brooklyn, and earned a living as an insurance saleswoman.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: In this interview, Po Yee Ung recounts her professional life: a Hong Kong career in business, and a New York City career in journalism. Ung evaluates Eighth Avenue's potential as a business and cultural center for Chinese immigrants in Brooklyn, and its deficiencies as a community resource. Ung recounts instances of petty neighborhood crime. She describes the neighborhood's aesthetic and real estate conditions. She talks about her affinity for Spanish-speaking sections of the neighborhood, particularly Hispanic food, customs, and people. Throughout the interview, Ung declines to answer questions that she views as too personal or that may indicate her general whereabouts. Interview in Cantonese conducted by Ka-Kam Chui.

dao.xlink:title: Oral History Interview (Audio) with Chun Wai (Billy) Wong

daodesc.p: Oral History Interview (Audio) with Chun Wai (Billy) Wong

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Chun Wai (Billy) Wong was born in Hong Kong, China, in 1964. He came to the United States with his family in 1984, and they settled in the Sunset Park neighborhood of Brooklyn. His grandparents had come to America years earlier, and they helped the family immigrate. Wong's mother worked in factories in the Chinatown neighborhood of Manhattan, and his father worked in a restaurant in the Bay Ridge neighborhood of Brooklyn. Wong worked as a youth Counselor and trip coordinator for youth in an after-school program at a public middle school. He also studied computer science at Brooklyn College and then transferred to City College. He worked as a computer programmer for one year after graduation. While working part-time as a youth counselor and studying at Hunter College, Wong began teaching dance in the after-school program at P.S. 220.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: In this interview, Chun Wai (Billy) Wong discusses his arrival in America and living in the Sunset Park neighborhood of Brooklyn compared to his birthplace of Hong Kong, China. He cites reasons why he likes living in New York. Wong describes the culture and lifestyle of the working Chinese community, the differences between Mainland Chinese people and Chinese people from Hong Kong, and the means by which Chinese-Americans travel back and forth between the neighborhoods, shop at food stores, and bank. Wong evaluates the economic changes in the two communities, new garment factories in Brooklyn, unionization, and health insurance in the factories in New York. Wong speaks about his job as a public school youth counselor, the characteristics of different groups of Chinese youth, and problems with gang activity. He recalls his education as a computer programmer at the City University of New York and his work after graduation. He recounts his shift from working as a youth counselor to studying dance at Hunter College. Wong discusses Chinese-American family life, including home ownership and the celebration of various American holidays within their community. He also sees changes in Sunset Park as it becomes more populated. Interview conducted by Mary Lui.

dao.xlink:title: Oral History Interview (Audio) with Reverend Fook (Samuel) Wong

daodesc.p: Oral History Interview (Audio) with Reverend Fook (Samuel) Wong

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Called by a relative to bring the word of Jesus Christ to Brooklyn's Chinese community, Reverend Fook (Samuel) Wong immigrated with his wife to New York City from Hong Kong in 1982, when he was forty-two years old. In 1983, they established the Chinese Promise Baptist Church in the Sunset Park neighborhood of Brooklyn. Through the church, Wong and his wife founded an annual Memorial Day Weekend street festival, which they hoped would increase neighborhood vitality and pride, while reducing street crime. At the time of the 1993 interview, Wong's daily activities were spent in community service: preaching, providing charity, organizing the annual street festival, and tending to the needs of his fifty parishioners.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: In this interview, Reverend Fook (Samuel) Wong discusses his life as an evangelical Baptist preacher in the Sunset Park neighborhood of Brooklyn. He talks about the innermost lives of the immigrant Chinese community he works with daily, detailing their "stages of immigration" as they assimilate into American culture. He details the domestic and financial challenges faced by overworked immigrants. Wong speaks on other issues, including corrupted youth, Chinese gang-related activities, the diversity of Brooklyn's Chinatown, and ethnic tensions between the area's Hispanic and Chinese residents. He also describes the Fuzhou people of China. Interview in Cantonese conducted by Mary Lui.

dao.xlink:title: Oral History Interview with Guillermo Wong and Norma Wong

daodesc.p: Oral History Interview with Guillermo Wong and Norma Wong

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Married couple Guillermo and Norma Wong immigrated to Brooklyn, New York, from Lima, Peru, in 1980 to escape political, social, and economic unrest. Because Guillermo Wong (who was half-Chinese) and Norma Wong both greatly appreciated Chinese culture, the Wong family felt at ease on their mixed Latino-Chinese street in the Sunset Park neighborhood of Brooklyn. At the time of the 1994 interview, the Wongs had been married twenty-four years, had three sons, and felt assimilated into American culture. They had recently launched a network marketing business, which they hoped would offer financial freedom and the opportunity to buy a home.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: In this interview, Guillermo and Norma Wong discuss their unique situation as a Chinese-Peruvian family living in a Chinese-Latino section of the Sunset Park neighborhood of Brooklyn, facing anti-Chinese slurs made by Latino residents, for example. They describe the ethnic makeup of their neighborhood, the politics of ethnic identity, racism, and discrimination. The Wongs recall the challenges of making it in America, including the process of finding housing, learning English, finding a job, and understanding American culture. Interview conducted by Gregory Ruf and Fabiana Chiu.

dao.xlink:title: Oral History Interview (Audio) with Yu Rong Zhu

daodesc.p: Oral History Interview (Audio) with Yu Rong Zhu

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Yu Rong Zhu, born in 1927 in Taishan, China, lived through the Japanese Invasion of China, the Chinese Civil War, and the Cultural Revolution. Zhu's status as a civil engineer protected him from governmental persecution, and he was satisfied with life. But his children, who lost educational and professional opportunities to the Cultural Revolution, urged him to immigrate to America. Zhu moved to San Francisco in 1988 with his wife, and his children soon followed. At the time of the interview, Zhu was sixty-six years old and lived in the Sunset Park neighborhood of Brooklyn with his wife and eldest daughter.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: In this interview, Yu Rong Zhu discusses his first sixty years of life, in which he witnessed the Japanese Invasion, the Chinese Civil War, and the Cultural Revolution. He reflects on the decision to move to America, his first two years in San Francisco, and his subsequent move to Brooklyn's Chinatown (Sunset Park). Zhu compares the lifestyle, living conditions, and apartment rental situation in the Chinatown communities of Brooklyn and Manhattan. He considers neighborhood crime, inter-ethnic relations, and the difficulties of life as a Chinese immigrant. Interview in Cantonese conducted by Mary Lui.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series 2: Photographs, 1993–1996, is comprised of photographs gathered for display at Brooklyn Historical Society's 1996 exhibition, New Neighbors: Sunset Park's Chinese Community, and Chinatown History Museum's 1999 exhibition,A Good Place to Land One's Feet: Brooklyn's New Chinese Community, as well as portraits of oral history interviewees, object and event photos, photo negatives, and slides. The series includes photos of the 1993 Sunset Park Lunar New Year parade and celebration, the Brooklyn Chinese-American Association 5th Anniversary celebration, Sunset Park community events, Eighth Avenue storefronts, and the day-to-day life of Sunset Park residents. Photographer Nina Kuo took the majority of the photos in this series. Materials from photographers Rex Chen and Charleen Chan are primarily photo negatives.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This series is arranged in six sub-series:

This arrangement maintains the order in which the materials were arranged when accessioned by the Brooklyn Historical Society: Sub-series 1 through 4 are arranged by photographer, and 5 and 6 are arranged by subject.

processinfo.head: Processing Note
processinfo.p: Duplicate photos were removed and added to the ARC.288 Brooklyn Historical Society institutional records.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Sub-series 2.1: Nina Kuo, 1993–1994, includes photographs taken by Kuo for the Brooklyn Historical Society's 1996 exhibition, New Neighbors: Sunset Park's Chinese Community, and the Chinatown History Museum's 1999 exhibition,A Good Place to Land One's Feet: Brooklyn's New Chinese Community. The Brooklyn Historical Society hired Kuo to photograph cultural and community events in Sunset Park, including the 1993 Lunar New Year parade and celebration, the Brooklyn Chinese-American 5th-anniversary dinner, and the Home in Sunset Park community event. This sub-series also includes photo negatives.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: The prints in this sub-series are arranged into folders chronologically by event. Each event's photographs are maintained in the original order imposed by the photographer. The negatives are housed separately.

processinfo.head: Processing Note
processinfo.p: File names provided by the photographer were maintained. The archivist used institutional documentation to determine the events, subjects, and dates of the photographs.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Sub-series 2.2: Rex Chen, 1994, includes photographs taken by Rex Chen for the Brooklyn Historical Society's 1996 exhibition, New Neighbors: Sunset Park's Chinese Community, and the Chinatown History Museum's 1999 exhibition,A Good Place to Land One's Feet: Brooklyn's New Chinese Community. These photos include shots of Sunset Park residents walking and shopping along Eighth Avenue, bilingual education classes at PS 314, and community events. The majority of the sub-series is comprised of photo negatives.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This sub-series is arranged by format.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Sub-series 2.3: Charleen Chan, 1994, includes photographs and photo negatives taken by photographer Charleen Chan for the Brooklyn Historical Society's 1996 exhibition, New Neighbors: Sunset Park's Chinese Community, and the Chinatown History Museum's 1999 exhibition,A Good Place to Land One's Feet: Brooklyn's New Chinese Community. These photographs include portraits of Sunset Park residents. Photo negatives make up the majority of this sub-series.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This sub-series is arranged by subject and format.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Sub-series 2.4: Unknown photographer, 1993-1994, includes photographs taken for the Brooklyn Historical Society's 1996 exhibition, New Neighbors: Sunset Park's Chinese Community, and the Chinatown History Museum's 1999 exhibition,A Good Place to Land One's Feet: Brooklyn's New Chinese Community. These photographs include exterior and interior shots of storefronts along Eighth Avenue in Sunset Park, a Sunset Park Lunar New Year celebration, Sunset Park residents walking on and around Eighth Avenue, and 8x10 prints of historical photographs used in the exhibition.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This sub-series is arranged by subject or event photographed.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Sub-series 2.5: [Portraits of oral history interviewees], 1994, includes 5x7 portraits taken of the interviewees for the original Sunset Park oral history project.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This sub-series is arranged alphabetically by subject name and includes the photographer. Portraits are numbered, and the names of each subject can be found in an item list within the binder.

separatedmaterial.head: Separated Materials
separatedmaterial.p: Internal documentation shows that every oral history interviewee was photographed, however, not all were found in this collection. The location of the following portraits, along with portraits of anonymized interviewees, is unknown:
6. Ning Yuan Li, by Rex Chen

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Sub-series 2.6: [Event and object photographs], 1996, includes photographs and photo negatives taken at the opening of Brooklyn Historical Society's exhibition, New Neighbors: Sunset Park's Chinese Community, in 1996, as well as 35mm slides of objects displayed in the exhibition.

did.langmaterial: Materials are in Mandarin, Cantonese, English, Spanish, and Turkish.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series 3: Donated materials, 1989–1996, includes materials donated to the Brooklyn Historical Society for the New Neighbors: Sunset Park's Chinese Community exhibition, also used in the Chinatown History Museum'sA Good Place to Land One's Feet: Brooklyn's New Chinese Community. Materials include ephemera and promotional materials from Sunset Park businesses, religious pamphlets and literature from local churches and temples, Chinese-language newspapers, promotional materials for cultural and arts organizations, and posters and flyers relating to Sunset Park cultural events. The majority of the materials were donated by Gregory Ruf and Ka Kam Chui, both of whom assisted with the exhibition and conducted interviews for the oral history portion of the project.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Series 3: Donated materials, 1989–1996, is arranged in 7 sub-series:

otherfindaid.head: Other Finding Aids
otherfindaid.p: An item list of donated materials can be found in the accession file.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Exhibition planners listed all donated materials, however, only a portion of these materials are still extant in the collection.

did.langmaterial: Materials are in Mandarin, Cantonese, and English.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Sub-Series 3.1: Gregory Ruf, 1989–1994, consists of materials donated by Gregory Ruf, including published works about Sunset Park, Chinese-language TV and entertainment guides, movie posters, Chinese-language newspapers and newsletters, promotional materials from Sunset Park businesses, Chinese Christian Mission literature and newsletters, and Brooklyn Chinese-American Association flyers and pamphlets. Gregory Ruf was a Coordinator for the oral history project and exhibition and conducted oral history interviews.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Sub-Series 3.1: Gregory Ruf, 1989–1994, is arranged into six files based on the subject of the materials. This arrangement was imposed by the Brooklyn Historical Society during their exhibition planning and was maintained by the archivist.

did.materialspec: Materials in Mandarin, Cantonese, and English.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Sub-Series 3.2: Ka Kam Chui, 1990 - 1994, consists of materials donated by Ka Kam Chui, including Chinese-language flyers and business advertisements; Chinese-language newspapers and newsletters; Sunset Park restaurant menus; Brooklyn and Manhattan transit tickets and maps; flyers, advertisements, and mailers for cultural and professional organizations, such as the Asian American Arts Centre and the National Association of Asian American Professionals; tickets and programs for cultural events; and religious literature and fundraising materials from religious organizations, such as the Chinese Christian Mission and the Ambassadors for Christ. Ka Kam Chui was a consultant and lead interviewer for the oral history portion of the project and donated the majority of materials for the Brooklyn Historical Society and Chinatown History Museum exhibitions.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Sub-Series 3.2: Ka Kam Chui, 1990 - 1994, is arranged into four files based on the subject of the materials. This arrangement was imposed by the Brooklyn Historical Society during their exhibition planning and was maintained by the archivist.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Sub-Series 3.7 Additional Donations, 1981-1996, includes materials from unknown donors, such as Chinese-language newspapers and newsletters, Lunar New Year posters, artwork, and trading cards. This file also includes materials relating to the Sunset Park Turkish community.
